question_title: What will U.S. Customs and Border Protection report as the number of Southwest Land Border Encounters in March 2026?
summary:
Forecasting March 2026 Southwest Land Border Encounters based on official CBP CSV data (FY2023-FY2026 FYTD).

Key data: FY2026 total SW border encounters have declined steadily: Oct 11,704 → Nov 10,957 → Dec 9,991 → Jan 9,726. The decline is decelerating (-6.4%, -8.8%, -2.7% MoM). Under the enforcement regime (Feb 2025+), the range has been 7,822-12,450.

The forecast centers around ~9,200 for March 2026, reflecting: (1) the ongoing declining trend from FY2026, (2) March having 31 days (vs Feb's 28), partially offsetting any daily-rate decline, (3) the White House reporting February 2026 as "lowest in history," suggesting the low-encounter regime persists, (4) potential for modest spring seasonal uptick, and (5) stable enforcement policies.

The distribution is mildly right-skewed to capture upside tail risk from seasonal spring migration or policy disruptions, while the downside reflects continued decline toward the FY2025 floor of ~7,800.

factors.description: Confirmed FY2026 declining trend: Oct 11,704 → Nov 10,957 → Dec 9,991 → Jan 9,726 (official CBP CSV data). Consistent downward trajectory with decelerating pace (-6.4%, -8.8%, -2.7% MoM).
FY2025 enforcement-era range (Feb-Sep 2025): 7,822 to 12,450 total SW encounters. Current levels (9,726) are in the middle of this range, not near the floor.
White House reported February 2026 as 'lowest in history' for border encounters, suggesting continued decline from January's 9,726.
factors.conditional: If 'lowest in history' applies to total SW border encounters specifically

factors.description: March has 31 days vs February's 28, so all else equal, March would be ~11% higher than February just from day count. This partially offsets any declining daily rate.
FY2025 precedent: March 2025 was 11,011 (6% below February's 11,710). Under same enforcement regime, March was somewhat lower than February.
Seasonal spring migration typically increases Feb-Apr. Under FY2025 enforcement, Apr-May were higher than Feb-Mar. May be partially suppressed under stronger FY2026 enforcement.
Status quo persistence: enforcement policies are stable, no major court orders or policy changes imminent. The low-encounter regime is unlikely to shift dramatically in March.
Tail risk: possible court intervention, policy change, or border crisis event could cause significant deviation from recent trends.

reasoning:
**Forecast: March 2026 Southwest Land Border Encounters**

| Percentile | Value |
|---|---|
| P10 | 7,600 |
| P20 | 8,200 |
| P40 | 8,900 |
| P60 | 9,500 |
| P80 | 10,200 |
| P90 | 11,000 |

**Key findings from research:**

- **Official CBP data** (downloaded CSV): FY2026 total SW border encounters have been declining steadily: Oct 11,704 → Nov 10,957 → Dec 9,991 → Jan 9,726
- The decline is **decelerating** (-6.4%, -8.8%, -2.7% month-over-month)
- Under the full enforcement regime (Feb 2025+), the range has been **7,822–12,450**
- February 2026 was reported as **"lowest in history"** by the White House
- March has 31 days vs February's 28, which partially offsets daily-rate declines
- The median forecast (~9,200) represents modest continued decline from January, consistent with the decelerating trend and stable enforcement policies

resolution_criteria: This question resolves to the numeric value of “Southwest Land Border Encounters” for March 2026 as reported by CBP on its Southwest Land Border Encounters statistics page. The primary source is CBP’s statistics page at [https://www.cbp.gov/newsroom/stats/southwest-land-border-encounters](https://www.cbp.gov/newsroom/stats/southwest-land-border-encounters)
fine_print: If the primary page is unavailable at resolution time, resolve using credible sources per [https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions)
background_info:
Monthly Southwest border encounter totals are a key operational indicator for U.S. migration enforcement, humanitarian capacity, and political risk.
